content.assessment: This proposal is the closest structural analogue to Poland's 1999 reform: both eliminate early selection points and converge tracking toward a single later transition. The Polish record offers genuine, if imperfectly portable, evidence that this structural mechanism is associated with system-wide PISA gains concentrated among previously lower-tracked students. However, the Polish record equally documents that structural reforms of this magnitude carry severe reversibility and implementation-sequencing risks: the 2017 reversal required closing roughly 7,000 schools, displaced up to 10,000 teachers, and produced acute placement failures for thousands of students in a single transition year. This suggests that the distributional gains achievable via delayed selection are real but contingent on implementation sequencing that avoids double-cohort transition shocks, an implementation design question this proposal does not yet resolve.
opportunities: Could design transition cohorts and school-closure sequencing explicitly informed by Poland's documented implementation failures to avoid repeating the double-cohort placement crisis
strengths: Directly mirrors the structural mechanism (delayed tracking, single entry point) credited with Poland's PISA gains, particularly for previously vocational-track students [F-live-polish_reform-04]
Structural convergence toward a single selection point matches the lens's criterion of eliminating multi-stage selection funnels
threats: Reversibility risk: Poland's history shows single-structure reforms are politically reversible under changed government, meaning any gains could be structurally undone within roughly two decades, as occurred in Poland's own case [F-live-polish_reform-05]
Distributional gains are not guaranteed to be portable, since Polish estimates reflect a distinct political and institutional context
weaknesses: Reform of this scale carries high implementation-sequencing risk; Poland's own transition (in reverse) produced thousands of unplaced students and massive school/teacher disruption, an outcome that could recur in either direction of structural change [F-live-polish_reform-05]

Comprehensive trend data validating reform effects only became available years after implementation in Poland (2022 for a 2017 reform), implying this reform's benefits may not be legible on short political timeframes [F-live-polish_reform-07]
